  :                            304 
  :                            305 				. the stack of four-word task descriptors
  :                            306 				. grows downwards from the position below
  :                            307 				. the lowest possible big bank descriptor
  :                            308 
  :                            309 				. the lowest big-bank pointer

  :                            326 				. *next points to next_descriptor
  :                            327 				. for small banks
  :                            328 				. this is the next available two-word
  :                            329 				. descriptor in the upward-growing
  :                            330 				. small banks decriptor list
  :                            331 
  :                            332 				. *next and the initial descriptor
  :                            333 				. where *next points are constructed
  :                            334 				. here at system start, so all
  :                            335 				. system images are restartable
  :                            336 				. and may be in ROM
  :                            337 
  :                            338 				. The buffer pointer in the initial
  :                            339 				. *next descriptor  points to the
  :                            340 				. first available RAM page after
  :                            341 				. the kernel ROM or kernel load
  :                            342 
  :                            343 				. there are 64 free-chains into the
  :                            344 				. "small" banks heap and one free-chain 
  :                            345 				. into the big-banks stack
  :                            346 	
  :                            347 				. The small-banks free-chains are one
  :                            348 				. per size, 1..64 pages (4096 words
  :                            349 				. to 262144 words)
  :                            350 
  :                            351 				. a "small" bank of 262144 words is
  :                            352 				. possible, because a smaller bank
  :                            353 				. may get bigger if the next assignment
  :                            354 				. does not fit in the same 262144-word
  :                            355 				. physical bank
  :                            356 
  :                            357 				. small banks are assigned from low-order
  :                            358 				. locations upwards. The free chains
  :                            359 				. reassign free storage at the lowest
  :                            360 				. addresses first.
  :                            361 
  :                            362 				. Freed space above the highest
  :                            363 				. assigned small bank is recombined
  :                            364 				. as single free area between the
  :                            365 				. the stack of small banks which grows
  :                            366 				. upwards and compresses downwards, and
  :                            367 				. the stack of big banks, which grows
  :                            368 				. downwards and compresses upwards
  :                            369 
  :                            370 				. There is one large-banks free-chain
  :                            371 				. for all sizes. A big bank may combine
  :                            372 				. one to nearly 65536 adjacent physical
  :                            373 				. banks of 262144 words
  :                            374 
  :                            375 				. A single large bank may contain nearly
  :                            376 				. 1024 times the 16 Megaword Address space
  :                            377 				.          -16 gigawords in total-
  :                            378 				. for large arrays which need to be contiguous
  :                            379 
  :                            380 				. An array needs to be contiguous if
  :                            381 				. multiword operands to single instructions
  :                            382 				. straddle physical 256K bank boundaries.
  :                            383 
  :                            384 				. Developers can also structure arrays
  :                            385 				. so that quad or double word operands
  :                            386 				. are safely inside one bank, even if
  :                            387 				. the containing structure straddles two
  :                            388 				. non-contiguous banks addressed with
  :                            389 				. two adjacent relocation base-registers
  :                            390 
  :                            391 				. Operand EA is translated to an absolute
  :                            392 				. address once for each operand read or write
  :                            393 
  :                            394 				. However very large contiguous bank
  :                            395 				. arrays are obtained with one ARRAY$ call
  :                            396 				. and accessed with one bank handle
  :                            397 
  :                            398 				. Arrays larger than the address space
  :                            399 				. are addressed a part at
  :                            400 				. a time. Structures straddling bank boundaries
  :                            401 				. are accessed using two adjacent base registers
  :                            402 				. to address two adjacent banks
  :                            403 
  :                            404 	.

  :                            301 .	starting at handle(1)

  :                            304 .	TCP connection and is the encoded offset of the
  :                            305 .	control block in the containing bank

  :                            415 .	these are here to read or write
  :                            416 .	a pointer in the kernel vector bank
  :                            417 
  :                            418 .	from routines which might be called
  :                            419 .	from either a kernel task or an

  :                            422 .	see$2 makes the kernel vector bank
  :                            423 .	visible instead of the application
  :                            424 .	vector bank
  :                            425

  :                            427 .	whichever it was before

  :                            202

00:00206A 3E0423              +212 	lcal	icmp			.

  :                            1 .       
  :                            2 .    Copyright Tim Cox, 2012
  :                            3 .    TimMilesCox@gmx.ch
  :                            4 .
  :                            5 .    This file is instruction code for the freeware processor architecture
  :                            6 .
  :                            7 .                RTA1
  :                            8 .
  :                            9 .    RTA1 is a free processor architecture specification.
  :                            10 .
  :                            11 .    The executable emulation of RTA1 is free software.
  :                            12 .
  :                            13 .    This instruction code for the target RTA1 architecture is free software
  :                            14 .
  :                            15 .    Software programs delivered with this software to connect the
  :                            16 .    emulated RTA1 with real network interfaces in the emulator host
  :                            17 .    are free software
  :                            18 .
  :                            19 .    Scripts and programs delivered with this software for running
  :                            20 .    on other computers and interacting with the RTA1 are free software
  :                            21 .
  :                            22 .    Scripts and utility programs for constructing RTA1 target
  :                            23 .    executable software are free software      
  :                            24 .
  :                            25 .    You can redistribute it and/or modify RTA1
  :                            26 .    under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by
  :                            27 .    the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
  :                            28 .    (at your option) any later version.
  :                            29 .
  :                            30 .    RTA1 is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
  :                            31 .    but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
  :                            32 .    MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
  :                            33 .    GNU General Public License for more details.
  :                            34 .
  :                            35 .    You should have received a copy of the GNU General Public License
  :                            36 .    along with RTA1.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
  :                            37 .
  :                            38 .
  :                            39 
  :                            40 . Emulation implements one kind of network interface
  :                            41 
  :                            42 . this can be modified when there is more than one interface type
  :                            43 . initially the only known type is modelled as a dual ported memory
  :                            44 . interface to the system
  :                            45 .
  :                            46 . half of the interfacing memory presents input frames
  :                            47 . the other half is available for output frames
  :                            48 
  :                            49 
  :                            50 .		_________________________________
  :                            51 .	>------>|1st receive buffer		|
  :                            52 .	|	|2nd				|
  :                            53 .	|	|				|
  :                            54 .	^
  :                            55 .	|
  :                            56 .	<-------|last receive buffer		|
  :                            57 .		|_______________________________|
  :                            58 .	>------>|1st transmit buffer		|
  :                            59 .	|	|2nd				|
  :                            60 .	|	|				|
  :                            61 .	^
  :                            62 .	|
  :                            63 .	<-------|last transmit buffer		|
  :                            64 .		|_______________________________|
  :                            65 
  :                            66 
  :                            67 . the frames arrive and are sent in cyclic order of the buffers
  :                            68 .
  :                            69 . there is an 8-word length + ethernet header at the start of each buffer
  :                            70 . this sketch represents a quartet of bits per symbol
  :                            71 
  :                            72 .	 00LLLL 00xxxx 00xxxx 00xxxx 00yyyy 00yyyy 00yyyy 00pppp
  :                            73 
  :                            74 .	________________________________________________________________________
  :                            75 .	| 00LLLL | 00xxxx | 00xxxx | 00xxxx | 00yyyy | 00yyyy | 00yyyy | 00pppp|
  :                            76 .	|________|________|________|________|________|________|________|_______|
  :                            77 .	| 00data | 00....
  :                            78 
  :                            79 
  :                            80 . network data is buffered 16 bits per computer word right up to the socket
  :                            81 . and XDR interfaces. These "interface dual ported" memories may be 16-bit
  :                            82 . memories reading out eight zero bits at the high order positions of each
  :                            83 . computer word, or they may be 24-bit memories: then the high-order 8 bits
  :                            84 . must be maintained with zero values
  :                            85 
  :                            86 .	LLLL	is frame size, and nonzero value written here indicates
  :                            87 .		a complete frame received, or available for transmission
  :                            88 .
  :                            89 .	xxxx xxxx xxxx	is the destination ethernet address
  :                            90 
  :                            91 .	yyyy yyyy yyyy	is the source ethernet address
  :                            92 
  :                            93 .	pppp	is the protocol family indicator, 0800 for IPv4, 0806 for ARP
  :                            94 .		as many as required
  :                            95 
  :                            96 
  :                            97 . the buffers are equal sized and used cyclically
  :                            98 
  :                            99 . the input machine signals to software that a frame is available
  :                            100 . by writing a length in bits 15:0 of the first word in the buffer:
  :                            101 .
  :                            102 .		0000 0000 LLLL LLLL LLLL LLLL
  :                            103 .
  :                            104 . The length is not self-inclusive. It is the frame length.
  :                            105 . The frame starts at the next word
  :                            106 
  :                            107 . software indicates that the frame has been sampled and the buffer is
  :                            108 . free by writing zero to this length word
  :                            109 
  :                            110 . software indicates that a frame for transmission is assembled 
  :                            111 . in the transmission buffer by writing a length in bits 15:0 of
  :                            112 . the first word in the transmission buffer. The length is the
  :                            113 . frame length and not self-inclusive
  :                            114 
  :                            115 . The frame for transmission starts in the second word of the
  :                            116 . transmission buffer
  :                            117 
  :                            118 . Transmission completion is indicated back to the software by zero
  :                            119 . in the length word, and this also indicates the transmission buffer
  :                            120 . is again free
  :                            121 
  :                            122 . This interface model is not part of RTA architecture but supports
  :                            123 . this emulation and is intended to work like network interfaces are 
  :                            124 . intended for RTA systems
  :                            125 . 
  :                            126 . That is, some of the 63 DEVICEs (arrays outside system memory)
  :                            127 . should carry network data to and from an RTA system.
  :                            128 
  :                            129 . A DEVICE is up to 65536 BANKS of up to 262144 WORDS of up to 24 bits
  :                            130 .
  :                            131 
  :                            132 . device 0 is system memory or executable space and is up to 16 gigawords
  :                            133 . of 24-bit memory
  :                            134 
  :                            135 . only system memory is selectable in pages of 4096 words as well as in
  :                            136 . banks of 256K
  :                            137 
  :                            138 . Memory in the other 63 possible devices is selectable in banks of
  :                            139 . 262144 words only
  :                            140 
  :                            141 . the pointer to a page of system memory based in one of the 72 relocation
  :                            142 . registers has the layout
  :                            143 
  :                            144 .	00pp pppp pppp pppp pppp pppp
  :                            145 
  :                            146 . where bit 22 = 0 indicates a 22-bit page pointer and the task has a granule
  :                            147 . of 4096 words addressed and available
  :                            148 
  :                            149 . the pointer to a bank of system memory in a relocation register has the layout
  :                            150 
  :                            151 .	01pp pppp pppp pppp pp00 0000
  :                            152 
  :                            153 . where bit 22 = 1 indicates a 16-bit bank pointer and the task has a granule
  :                            154 . of 262144 words addressed and available
  :                            155 
  :                            156 . bits 5:0 = 00 0000 indicate that the bank is in system memory
  :                            157 .
  :                            158 . the pointer to a bank of "I/O Memory" (strictly an array other than
  :                            159 . system memory) has the layout in a relocation register
  :                            160 
  :                            161 .	01bb bbbb bbbb bbbb bbDE VICE
  :                            162 
  :                            163 . where bit 22 = 1 = Big Bank ** DEVICE = nonzero = some other device than
  :                            164 . system memory
  :                            165 
  :                            166 . Some of these 63 possible arrays outside system memory will be battery-
  :                            167 . supported SRAMS of 24-bit words containing realtime transaction file systems
  :                            168 
  :                            169 . Others may be flash memories containing default system images and occasional
  :                            170 . data backups (but flash memories may not be used for high-frequency
  :                            171 . transaction update data)
  :                            172 
  :                            173 . some may be insertable flashes containing 16- or 8-bit memories organised
  :                            174 . in a file system architecture readable and writable by other systems
  :                            175 
  :                            176 . some may be video RAMs of up to 16 Gigawords of 24-bit words
  :                            177 
  :                            178 . At least one device must be an array interfacing a network adaptor to the
  :                            179 . RTA system with 16 network data bits per computer word as modelled here
  :                            180 
  :                            181 . This interface array is supplied to the emulator by a Unix shared memory
  :                            182 . block of 524288 bytes which looks to RTA like a device of one bank of
  :                            183 . 262144 16-bit words.
  :                            184 
  :                            185 . RTA reads each word out as a 24-bit computer word with bits 23:16 = 0000 0000
  :                            186 . The first half of this block is receive buffers and the second half transmit
  :                            187 . buffers
  :                            188 
  :                            189 . the settings here are that RPAGEW is 1024 words. This is the buffer size
  :                            190 . and it allows an ethernet frame of 1514+ bytes. Jumbos are not accounted
  :                            191 . for in the emulation. A device in a large-configuration RTA may have
  :                            192 . 65536 * 262144 words in a network interface device, but the emulator has
  :                            193 . only 1 * 262144 words in its one network interface space.
  :                            194 
  :                            195 . Therefore the emulator will only test standard ethernet traffic
  :                            196 
  :                            197 . the configuration of the emulated interface (which links to a real interface
  :                            198 . outside the emulator) is therefore
  :                            199 
  :                            200 .	128 receive buffers of 1024 words each
  :                            201 . plus	128 transmit buffers of 1024 words each
  :                            202 
  :                            203 
  :                            204 . Notes. Note 1
  :                            205 
  :                            206 . To avoid accidental damage, device banks (as distinct from system memory
  :                            207 . banks) may only be based by interrupt code. This avoids inadvertant
  :                            208 . traversal of array accesses across all the devices. At its simplest,
  :                            209 . the internal interrupts (system calls II 64+32 .. II 64+63) only need
  :                            210 . to copy the bank handle from user-A register, base it, and return:
  :                            211 
  :                            212 .		la	A
  :                            213 .		sabr	32
  :                            214 .		ir	0
  :                            215 
  :                            216 		.
  :                            217 		.
  :                            218 
  :                            219 .		la	A
  :                            220 .		sabr	63
  :                            221 .		ir	0
  :                            222 
  :                            223 . but as much extra security can be added to that as wished in more complex OS
  :                            224 
  :                            225 . Once the storage area is based, the application can access it at will.
  :                            226 . If this is not wished, the interrupt code itself must carry out required
  :                            227 . operations and unbase the storage area before return. The simplest model
  :                            228 . here relies on trusted applications which base the sensitive banks
  :                            229 . by design and not by accident
  :                            230 
  :                            231 . This also provides the beginning of a security model for any implementation
  :                            232 . which includes the translatable storage handle
  :                            233 
  :                            234 . Where a handle 0xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx is absolute whether it points
  :                            235 . to system memory or a device
  :                            236 
  :                            237 . 1xxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx is reserved for a possible translating range of
  :                            238 . storage area handles, for moveable/swappable application storage.
  :                            239 . A hardware implementation which does translatable handles would reserve
  :                            240 . the basing of all absolute areas (device and system memory) to privileged
  :                            241 . code of some sort, perhaps system calls as in this initial model
  :                            242 
  :                            243 . applications could then only directly base an allowed set of areas described
  :                            244 . in their translate lists.
  :                            245 
  :                            246 
  :                            247 
  :                            248 . Note 2
  :                            249 
  :                            250 . the interface handler here will offer each frame to the protocol family
  :                            251 . (mostly IPv4 or similar), which must decide immediately whether to take
  :                            252 . the frame and place it in storage visible to the application.
  :                            253 
  :                            254 . This means IP will check IP correctness, and call ICMP, UDP or TCP which
  :                            255 . must checksum as necessary while copying the datagram to socket storage.
  :                            256 
  :                            257 . This is not a flat-address system and there is no zero-copy
  :                            258 . receive. But there is only one copy, and it is carried out during checksum
  :                            259 . generation. After that the datagrams are in socket-associated buffers which
  :                            260 . are placed in the address space of the application.
  :                            261 
  :                            262 . The socket or XDR interface will format the data when the application
  :                            263 . subsequently receives it over the socket API.
  :                            264 
  :                            265 . on return from the call to the protocol family, the interface buffer is
  :                            266 . freed
  :                            267 
  :                            268 
  :                            269 
  :                            270 RX_LL_HL $equ	d$save
  :                            271 
  :                            272 
  :                            273 
  :                            274 $(1)
  :                            275 
  :                            276 .	pointer array to the network interface descriptors
  :                            277 .	these are numbered starting from one as in MIB-II
  :                            278 .	interfaces table
  :                            279 
  :                            280 .	word zero of this table is a count of the number of
  :                            281 .	interface descriptors in use
  :                            282 
  :                            283 .	the initial value 1 indicates that there is one
  :                            284 .	interface so far, interface(1) = internal loopback,
  :                            285 .	and the next to be configured ought to be interface(2)
  :                            286 
  :                            287 .	to obtain a descriptor for a new interface, increment
  :                            288 .	the counter in the first word of the array and select
  :                            289 .	the pointer at
  :                            290 
  :                            291 .	i_f_pointer_array[i_f_pointer_array[0]]
  :                            292 
  :                            293 .	for example
  :                            294 .
  :                            295 .		lx		i_f_pointer_array
  :                            296 .		ax, xi		1
  :                            297 .		sx		i_f_pointer_array
  :                            298 .		lx		0, x
  :                            299 
  :                            300 
  :                            301 i_f_pointer_array	. who wants to go multiplying subscripts?

  :                            66 	jza	emergency_netbank
  :                            67 
  :                            68 	lx	(NET_BASE*/12)
  :                            69 .	sx	rx_buffers
  :                            70

  :                            72

  :                            105 	.	can we get 16K then?
  :                            106 
  :                            107 	la, xi	4096*4
  :                            108 	ii	BANK$

  :                            116 					. pointer to transmission buffers

  :                            152 
  :                            153 nowhere_to_go_netbank
  :                            154 
  :                            155 .	if we couldn't get 8K for network buffers
  :                            156 .	then we won't start any new tasks either
  :                            157 
  :                            158 .	we should maybe set an LED pattern
  :                            159 .	and exit the IP process
  :                            160 
  :                            161 .	otherwise
  :                            162 .	we could program a flash to say it's always full
  :                            163 .	and no buffer is ever available in it
  :                            164 
  :                            165 	lret	0

  :                            101 					. except the area containing instructions plus
  :                            102 					. static data / TCB / B1 frames / vector bank
  :                            103 					. which may make a quarter too much to ask
  :                            104 net_buffer_request

00:0021BA 750043              +106 	ii	BANK$
00:0021BB 380005              +107 	pop	b
00:0021BC D601C9              +108 	jnza	net_buffer_acquired
00:0021BD 0E0001              +109 	sbr	1			. halve the request until a bank
00:0021BE 600005              +110 	la	b			. is granted or is not
00:0021BF 480F27              +111 	lk	(-262144)		. does the request size remain # banks?
00:0021C0 AF0F28              +112 	mta	(0)			. ask again if so
00:0021C1 B601B9              +113 	j	net_buffer_request
00:0021C2 480F29              +114 	lk	(-65536)		. has the request size changed
00:0021C3 AF0F28              +115 	mta	(0)			. from banks to < 1 bank? Then
00:0021C4 600F2A              +116 	la	(65536)			. its size restarts at 16 pages
00:0021C5 4DF000              +117 	lk, xi	-4096			. has the request diminished
00:0021C6 AF0F28              +118 	mta	(0)			. to < 4096?

  :                            58 TX_DATAGRAML	$equ	11	. used in transmit = unfragmented datagram
  :                            59 				. generated in interfaces = xmitted frame
  :                            60 
  :                            61 DYNAMIC_IP_HEADER $equ	0
  :                            62 		. if IP header length cannot be known at socket layer
  :                            63 		. then make this a multiple of 4 > 20 and < 64
  :                            64 		. to give a fixed large space for IP to add header
  :                            65 		. options
  :                            66 
  :                            67 		. otherwise IP header size is fixed when the socket
  :                            68 		. layer acquires a buffer

  :                            300 
  :                            301 .	ipv4_rx, input driver for a system with fixed dual ported
  :                            302 .	interface buffers
  :                            303 
  :                            304 .	this is an upward call from routine frames
  :                            305 
  :                            306 .	copying to socket-visible buffers is combined with
  :                            307 .	checksumming
  :                            308 
  :                            309 .	each word in the interface buffers is read only once
  :                            310 
  :                            311 .	ipv4_rx pushes and checks the IP headers in the
  :                            312 .	internal stack, which is a register array
  :                            313 
  :                            314 .	upper protocols obtain a buffer, then pop the IP headers
  :                            315 .	into it ahead of their own protocol message for
  :                            316 .	checksumming during transfer
  :                            317

  :                            97 
  :                            98 .	this is the direct feed to udpq from interfaces which have
  :                            99 .	fixed dual-ported buffering. Interfaces with a direct access
  :                            100 .	to system memory would not have this packet copy
  :                            101 
  :                            102 .	The packet copy is used as an occasion to checksum
  :                            103 
  :                            104 .	The packet copy places the packets in rx_buffer with
  :                            105 .	T77 descriptors which are called ACWs
  :                            106 
  :                            107 .	This routine cannot be called for fragmented datagrams
  :                            108 .	because it is instant from frame readout to UDP checksum
  :                            109 .	generation
  :                            110 
  :                            111 .	this is an upward call from ipv4_rx
  :                            112

  :                            257 
  :                            258 .	this is a protocol which has a length in the header
  :                            259 .	so the first step is to see if the IP datagram is
  :                            260 .	long enough to contain the UDP datagram
  :                            261 .	after that the operative length is the one in
  :                            262 .	the header
  :                            263 
  :                            264 udp_rx_check
  :                            265 .	ds	LAST_BYTES	. LAST_BYTES:CHECKSUM
  :                            266 	lk	b
  :                            267 
  :                            268 	lr	a
  :                            269 	dl	6
  :                            270 	da	uugram:sourcep
  :                            271 	aa	uugram:udgraml
  :                            272 	ds	6
  :                            273 	la	r
  :                            274 
  :                            275 	qpop	0, x
  :                            276 	ax, xi	4
  :                            277 	sx	descriptor:apdata
  :                            278 
  :                            279 	lcal	udp_rx_payload
  :                            280 
  :                            281 	xorB	k
  :                            282 	jnzb	ipv4_drop
  :                            283 
  :                            284 	ly, xi	udpq
  :                            285 	la	fp
  :                            286 	lcal	q2
  :                            287 	
  :                            288 	j	ipv4_rxz
  :                            289 
  :                            290 	$endif
  :                            291 
  :                            292 .	this is the feed to udpq for reassembled datagrams
  :                            293 .	which are already in a chain of rx_buffer with ACW
  :                            294 .	descriptors
  :                            295 
  :                            296 .	each fragment has the checksum addition of its data
  :                            297 .	in the ACW, to exploit the chance of checksum
  :                            298 .	generation during copy to rx_buffer
  :                            299 
  :                            300 .	it has been second-guessed in all cases that checksum
  :                            301 .	is specified in the UDP header (the UDP header may not
  :                            302 .	be the first fragment of the datagram to arrive).
  :                            303 
  :                            304 .	also, it is unlikely that UDP payload large enough
  :                            305 .	to need reassembly would be exempt from checksum
  :                            306 
  :                            307 .	no-checksum is usually a feature of high-speed
  :                            308 .	media traffic which is transmitted in much smaller
  :                            309 .	packets than any MTU
  :                            310 
  :                            311 .	large reassembly is more likely to be traffic like
  :                            312 .	NFS in 8KByte slabs (or 3KByte slabs where RTA is the
  :                            313 .	NFS client). Checksum would certainly apply
  :                            314 
  :                            315 .	So all the data arriving here has been end-around
  :                            316 .	added and the positive sum left in the ACW
  :                            317 
  :                            318 .	if the UDP header has a nonzero checksum, the data sums
  :                            319 .	are now summed with network address, protocol index
  :                            320 .	and user datagram length, then inverted and if finally
  :                            321 .	zero inverted again. Then compared.
  :                            322 
  :                            323 	$if	RX_FRAGMENTS
  :                            324 udp_rx_rasm
  :                            325 	lx	descriptor:uproto
  :                            326 	la	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            327 	jza	udp_rx_rasm_nocheck
  :                            328 	push	a
  :                            329 	z	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            330 	lcal	rasm77_gcheck
  :                            331 	tnz	b		. because zero isn't
  :                            332 	xorB,xi	-1		. a UDP checksum
  :                            333 	xorB	0, sp
  :                            334 	pop	uugram:checksum, x
  :                            335 	la	fp
  :                            336 	jnzb	net_free_chain

  :                            341 	$endif

  :                            376 udp_txbx
00:0024D5 3D0000              +377 	fret	0
  :                            378 				. this is a far call because
  :                            379 				. socket users may call it
  :                            380 
  :                            381 				. unlike the similar calls
  :                            382 				. in icmp and tcp which are
  :                            383 				. not application visible
  :                            384 
  :                            385 
  :                            386 
  :                            387 . far calls
  :                            388 
  :                            389 .	udp_acw_send
  :                            390 .	udp_acw_send_nocheck
  :                            391 
  :                            392 . get to here
  :                            393 
  :                            394 . udp_txaw
  :                            395 . udp_txaw_nocheck
  :                            396 
  :                            397 . low level call with the acw adress in X
  :                            398 
  :                            399 . both ports must be in the UDP header
  :                            400 . both adresses must be in the address buffer
  :                            401 
  :                            402 . if there is no come-from address in the address buffer
  :                            403 . the thing cannot now be checksummed and an earlier
  :                            404 . call should have done the internal routing step to
  :                            405 . find the sending interface and its default network
  :                            406 . address
  :                            407 
  :                            408 . so in that case an error gets returned as described
  :                            409 . in the rest of this discussion
  :                            410 
  :                            411 . it's the interface number that is important in
  :                            412 . finding a local address for the datagram and
  :                            413 . for the checksum
  :                            414 
  :                            415 . it's also optimal if the interface index is already
  :                            416 . in the descriptor
  :                            417 
  :                            418 . we have the local address and the interface number
  :                            419 . already when we just bounce back reversing the
  :                            420 . incoming addresses as a server with the calls to
  :                            421 
  :                            422 . 	udp_rtxbuffer->udp_response_buffer
  :                            423 
  :                            424 . and after message construction, to here:
  :                            425 
  :                            426 .	udp_acw_send->udp_txaw
  :                            427 
  :                            428 . some implementations suggest you do a connect
  :                            429 . which makes a routing call to IP and plugs the
  :                            430 . default network address of the interface back
  :                            431 . into the socket
  :                            432 
  :                            433 . having a from address in the socket is
  :                            434 . usable when you are making the conventional
  :                            435 . calls send and sendto
  :                            436 
  :                            437 . the connect idea is nice for send but not
  :                            438 . for sendto which is meant for random desinations
  :                            439 . reached over various interfaces
  :                            440 
  :                            441 . it's also not right that an application should
  :                            442 . need to pick a local network address before
  :                            443 . binding a UDP socket. The application and its
  :                            444 . developer cannot know that unless the
  :                            445 . application is for one host with one interface
  :                            446 
  :                            447 . since this is the final path step of all the
  :                            448 . possible interface calls to send UDP
  :                            449 
  :                            450 . and the step where checksum is done if opted
  :                            451 . and the come-from address therefore needed
  :                            452 
  :                            453 . we'll do the following here
  :                            454 
  :                            455 . we'll return with an error code if there is
  :                            456 . no source address, because
  :                            457 
  :                            458 . if you got here with the primitive
  :                            459 
  :                            460 .	udp_acw_send
  :                            461 
  :                            462 . then it was up to you to copy the address
  :                            463 . information into the buffers. At this
  :                            464 . point it's no longer certain that the
  :                            465 . socket/port structure is based. That's
  :                            466 . not even in the parameters to this call
  :                            467 . and it's intended that the caller decides
  :                            468 . what at this point is based in socket
  :                            469 . space
  :                            470 
  :                            471 . so we'll send back an error
  :                            472 
  :                            473 . if you obtained the buffer with
  :                            474 
  :                            475 .	udp_rtxbuffer
  :                            476 
  :                            477 . then that DID set the addresses up for you
  :                            478 
  :                            479 . if you came from send, send had access
  :                            480 . to your socket and copied the addresses
  :                            481 . in there
  :                            482 
  :                            483 . if you then get to here with no come-from
  :                            484 . address you hadn't done the connect call
  :                            485 . which writes both to and from addresses
  :                            486 . to the socket. So the same error return
  :                            487 . is appropriate, we'll call it something
  :                            488 . like
  :                            489 
  :                            490 . UDP_NOSOURCENET	$equ	-5
  :                            491 
  :                            492 . if you got here from sendto, that makes
  :                            493 . the same internal routing call as connect
  :                            494 . for every datagram
  :                            495 . but writes what it finds to the datagram
  :                            496 . address buffer and not to the socket
  :                            497 
  :                            498 . recvfrom can cheekily write all the
  :                            499 . incoming addressing to the socket
  :                            500 
  :                            501 . then sendto can skip the internal routing
  :                            502 . step if the socket has a source address
  :                            503 . and the destinaton address stored on the
  :                            504 . socket is the sendto parameter address
  :                            505 
  :                            506 . send can just send on the recvfrom
  :                            507 . addresses as if it had connected
  :                            508 
  :                            509 . since the routing call finds the next hop
  :                            510 . as well as the interface, that information
  :                            511 . stays in the address buffer of the datagram
  :                            512 . and IP routing will not need to find the
  :                            513 . next hop again for this one datagram
  :                            514 
  :                            515 . for connect/send the next-hop isn't saved,
  :                            516 . because it might change. But the interface
  :                            517 . is saved
  :                            518 
  :                            519

01:000306 000785              +68 tcp_lsegment_noport + tcp_reset_deflect	. default vector RX large segment no port bound:
  :                            69 					. TCP_RST without preliminary checksum. Avoids
  :                            70 					. expensive input-completion / buffering / repooling
  :                            71 
  :                            72 					. can be changed administratively either to silent drop
  :                            73 					. or even to full checksum to choose whether
  :                            74 					. RST + discard or silent discard
  :                            75

  :                            226 		. in state TCP_TIMEWAIT during outgoing close.

  :                            232 		.	TCP_CLOSEWAIT
  :                            233 
  :                            234 		. because many application protocols use an inactivity timeout.
  :                            235 		. The timeout is signalled upwards by connection state change
  :                            236 		.
  :                            237 
  :                            238 		. The timeout is not active if connection:timer(1) is zero

  :                            876 
  :                            877 .	group of similar functions which
  :                            878 .	get a buffer and send a short segment
  :                            879 .
  :                            880 .	for	SYN
  :                            881 .		SYN_ACK
  :                            882 .		FIN
  :                            883 .		FIN_ACK
  :                            884 
  :                            885 .	connection address is in X
  :                            886 
  :                            887 .       hl_code is in A
  :                            888 .       if header is over 5 longwords
  :                            889 .       address of options list is in B
  :                            890 
  :                            891 .	options list is 16 bits per word
  :                            892 
  :                            893 .		0000 0000 tttt tttt llll llll
  :                            894 .		0000 0000 vvvv vvvv vvvv vvvv
  :                            895 
  :                            896 .	if there is payload
  :                            897 .	the byte count is in B
  :                            898 .	the data address is in Y
  :                            899 .	the data at Y is already in
  :                            900 .	16-bit network format
  :                            901 
  :                            902 .		0000 0000 xxxx xxxx yyyy yyyy
  :                            903 
  :                            904 .       connection state variables are
  :                            905 .       used
  :                            906 
  :                            907 .       the segment is queued and flagged for
  :                            908 .       retain, meaning IP doesn't pool the
  :                            909 .       buffer on transmit, but lets TCP do
  :                            910 .       that on remote ACK
  :                            911 
  :                            912 .	these are far calls visible to
  :                            913 .	applications
  :                            914 
  :                            915 .	the vector for tcp_respond_syn_ack
  :                            916 .	is tcp_accept
  :                            917 
  :                            918 .	the vector for tcp_send_i
  :                            919 .	is tcp_send_push
  :                            920 
  :                            921

  :                            1538 
  :                            1539 
  :                            1540 .	buffer queues which are shared between tasks have to be accessed
  :                            1541 .	in a protected way. They are mainly the queues which both the IP
  :                            1542 .	task and an application protocol access. Some application protocols
  :                            1543 .	are in the IP task and some are not
  :                            1544 
  :                            1545 .	it would be quite bad to place TS cells in every queue structure
  :                            1546 .	because not all queues need them. Sockets and connections are the
  :                            1547 .	best available place for these lock cells
  :                            1548 
  :                            1549 .	all this code is SMP, so the code
  :                            1550 
  :                            1551 .	1.	prevents itself from getting interrupted or switched
  :                            1552 .	2.	locks the connection
  :                            1553 .	3.	updates the queue
  :                            1554 .	4.	frees the lock
  :                            1555 .	5.	reloads the interrupt mask it had before
  :                            1556 
  :                            1557 
  :                            1558 .	tcp_write_rxq is called from TCP in the IP task
  :                            1559 
  :                            1560 tcp_write_rxq
  :                            1561

  :                            157 
  :                            158 
  :                            159 	.	0:	is the remote ACK possible?
  :                            160 
  :                            161 	.	0:5	is there a remote reset?
  :                            162 
  :                            163 	.	1:	cache remote ACK and window
  :                            164 
  :                            165 	.	2:1	send waiting segments as remote
  :                            166 	.		window allows
  :                            167 
  :                            168 	.	2:2	release segments queued for ACK
  :                            169 	.		according to remote ACK value
  :                            170 	.		vs sequence + size of sent output
  :                            171 
  :                            172 	.	3:	compare remote sequence with local
  :                            173 	.		ACK
  :                            174 
  :                            175 	.	4:	if less, shave the leading edge
  :                            176 	.		of the input
  :                            177 
  :                            178 	.	5:	if more but sequence + length
  :                            179 	.		within local ACK + window, deferq
  :                            180 	.		and otherwise drop
  :                            181 
  :                            182 	.	6:	accept as much of the net input at 4:
  :                            183 	.		as local window allowed. This avoids
  :                            184 	.		accepting single bytes which are
  :                            185 	.		sent to push the window open
  :                            186 	.		and also avoids rejecting payload
  :                            187 	.		for which we have advertised
  :                            188 	.		window. Drop the segment if no
  :                            189 	.		part of it is accepted, and shave
  :                            190 	.		the trailing edge if part of it
  :                            191 	.		is rejected
  :                            192 
  :                            193 	.		It might be unwise to accept beyond
  :                            194 	.		window. A well behaved remote system
  :                            195 	.		won't send beyond window (apart from
  :                            196 	.		experimental 1-byters, and the response
  :                            197 	.		to those is drop them and advertise
  :                            198 	.		new window). A malicious remote system
  :                            199 	.		might try to manipulate window
  :                            200 
  :                            201 	.	7:	Advance local ACK and reduce local
  :                            202 	.		window according to the amount accepted
  :                            203 
  :                            204 	.	8:	generate new local window
  :                            205 
  :                            206 	.	9:	transmit segments queued for remote window
  :                            207 	.		according to remote ACK + window,  and
  :                            208 	.		if there is none, and no further input
  :                            209 	.		segment after this has yet been received
  :                            210 	.		for this connection, send an ACK, unless
  :                            211 	.		the incoming segment is an ACK without
  :                            212 	.		payload and its SEQ is not beyond local ACK
  :                            213 
  :                            214 	.		SYN and FIN have a payload weight of
  :                            215 	.		1 byte
  :                            216 
  :                            217 	.		restart connection:timer(1)
  :                            218 	.		maybe do this at an early point, depending
  :                            219 	.		whether the attempt to communicate
  :                            220 	.		counts as keepalive even if it is
  :                            221 	.		out of range
  :                            222 
  :                            223 
  :                            224 
  :                            225 
  :                            226 	.	1	is the remote ACK possible?
  :                            227

  :                            41 
  :                            42 .	these blocking-only calls are central to the
  :                            43 .	known classical POSIX-like socket interface
  :                            44 
  :                            45 .	they correspond to the vectors	listen()
  :                            46 .					accept()
  :                            47 .					close()
  :                            48 .					connect()
  :                            49 
  :                            50 .	their blocking characteristic is in this file connect.msm
  :                            51 
  :                            52 .	and they complement		recv()
  :                            53 .	and				send()
  :                            54 
  :                            55 .	which block or not per flags argument
  :                            56 
  :                            57 .	first parameter of these routines is the handle integer
  :                            58 
  :                            59 .	the target connections of these routines are
  :                            60 .	in the default connections array
  :                            61 .	not the high-volume connection arrays
  :                            62 
  :                            63 
  :                            64

  :                            182 
  :                            183 .	for write to socket buffer
  :                            184 .	16 bits at a time are read from user data
  :                            185 .	via the staging register
  :                            186 
  :                            187 .	PSR designator b$streamZR is on
  :                            188 .	b$stream16r gets cleared here
  :                            189 .	this is for 24-bit opaque data
  :                            190 .	
  :                            191 
  :                            192 .	in TCP only
  :                            193 .	the caller may write an odd number of bytes
  :                            194 .	into the transmit segment buffer, so there
  :                            195 .	may be a data word half written from the 
  :                            196 .	previous send call
  :                            197 
  :                            198 .	the pointer is apdata[word] + bytes[byte]
  :                            199 .	descriptor:bytes is updated
  :                            200 
  :                            201 .	the segment may be filled and queued, and
  :                            202 .	a new segment started
  :                            203 
  :                            204 .	on calling, the connection is in x
  :                            205 .	the caller may have a dynamic frame on FP
  :                            206 .	we save fp and use it as descriptor:
  :                            207 
  :                            208 .	the user data is in q:RDATA:RDATAC
  :                            209 .	the user byte count is in a
  :                            210

  :                            718 XDR_TCP_INLINE $set 0
  :                            719 	$if	XDR_TCP_INLINE
  :                            720 
  :                            721 .	there is no detailed guidance from IETF, SUN or anywhere else
  :                            722 .	whether marked record fragments can split fields up
  :                            723 
  :                            724 .	Apple's NFS / TCP is written as if that was possible, so this
  :                            725 .	is also written as if a new record mark could start in the
  :                            726 .	middle of an opaque or number field
  :                            727 
  :                            728 .	these XDR calls will only work on a blocking connection
  :                            729 .	so the socket owner may not be the IP stack itself for these
  :                            730 
  :                            731 
  :                            732 
  :                            733 tcp_rbuffer_marked

  :                            735 	jzb	tcp_read_record_mark

  :                            737 	push,xi	0
  :                            738 	jc	tcp_rbuffer_record
  :                            739 	ana	RECORD_MARK
  :                            740 	sa	0, sp
  :                            741 	la	RECORD_MARK
  :                            742 	lb, xi	0
  :                            743

  :                            747 	pop	a
  :                            748 	jnza	tcp_read_record_mark
  :                            749 	wsr	16
  :                            750 	lret	0
  :                            751 
  :                            752 tcp_read_record_mark
  :                            753 	tp	RECORD_MARK_FLAG
  :                            754 	j	tcp_read_record_alignment_error
  :                            755 	push	a
  :                            756 	push	WDATA
  :                            757 	push	WDATAC
  :                            758 	push	p
  :                            759 	push,xi	6
  :                            760 	pop	p
  :                            761 	z	WDATAC
  :                            762 	la, xi	4
  :                            763 	lcal	tcp_rbuffer
  :                            764 	wsr	16
  :                            765 	dl	6
  :                            766 	dsa	16
  :                            767 	ds	RECORD_MARK_FLAG
  :                            768 	pop	p

  :                            778 
  :                            779 
  :                            780 tcp_read_opaque
  :                            781 	push	fp
  :                            782 	z	WDATAC
  :                            783 			. ask for the number of bytes you actually want
  :                            784 			. round up the descriptor:readpointer
  :                            785 			. in the final buffer read
  :                            786 			. push out the last delivered word to 24 bits
  :                            787 
  :                            788 	push	a	. how much you want
  :                            789 
  :                            790 	lcal	tcp_rbuffer_marked
  :                            791 	jnzb	tcp_incomplete_opaque_read
  :                            792 
  :                            793 
  :                            794 	z	WDATAC
  :                            795 
  :                            796 	la	0, sp	. # bytes requested
  :                            797 	and, xi	3	. flag up not 4 multiple
  :                            798 	jza	tcp_read_opaquex
  :                            799 
  :                            800 	tz	fp
  :                            801 	j	tcp_round_opaque_read
  :                            802 
  :                            803 			. we tested the marked record
  :                            804 			. for all present, so if no
  :                            805 			. segment buffer is at the
  :                            806 			. queue head, we are scanning
  :                            807 			. the wrong structure
  :                            808 
  :                            809 tcp_incomplete_opaque_read
  :                            810 	n	0, sp	. flag something is miscalculated
  :                            811 	j	tcp_read_opaquex
  :                            812 
  :                            813 tcp_round_opaque_read
  :                            814 	xor, xi	3
  :                            815 	push, i	1, a
  :                            816 	popA	descriptor:readpoint
  :                            817

  :                            943 udp_readx
00:002C71 350000              +944 	lret	0
  :                            945 
  :                            946 .	that routine consumes the buffers in the user datagram
  :                            947 .	unconditionally and uses the cursor :readpoint
  :                            948 .	it is for XDR / RPC field extractions
  :                            949 
  :                            950 .	next is a similar routine for plain udgram_read
  :                            951 .	the buffers are not dropped, in case it's flagged
  :                            952 .	that this is a PEEK
  :                            953 
  :                            954 .	the :readpoint cursor is not maintained or consulted

00:002D53 400005              +139 	lr	b
  :                            140 
  :                            141 .	now keep looking to see if the new heap top
  :                            142 .	buffer is on its free chain. If it is, it
  :                            143 .	is the one at the far end, because the
  :                            144 .	highest-positioned buffers are the last
  :                            145 .	choice for re-use
  :                            146 
  :                            147 .	the stack of descriptors grows down towards
  :                            148 .	the heap of buffers which grows up
  :                            149 
  :                            150 .	the stack of descriptors packs back up
  :                            151 .	away from the heap of buffers which
  :                            152 .	packs down
  :                            153 
  :                            154 
  :                            155

00:002D89 60B000              +252 readq	la	queue:head
00:002D8A C60D8D              +253 	jza	readqz
00:002D8B 68C000              +254 	lb	0, a
00:002D8C 28B000              +255 	sb	queue:head
00:002D8D 350000              +256 readqz	lret	0
  :                            257 
  :                            258         $if      QSIMULTANEOUS
  :                            259 
  :                            260 .	descriptors are short-term on two chains
  :                            261 .	so that TCP segments awaiting ACK
  :                            262 .	can be on the interface queue and the
  :                            263 .	connection's ACK wait queue at the same time
  :                            264 
  :                            265 .	TCP uses chain link descriptor:next and so does
  :                            266 .	almost all the t77.4 protocol suite
  :                            267 
  :                            268 .	interface queues use chain link descriptor:ifql
  :                            269 
  :                            270 .	ifql is a reuse of descriptor:readpoint which
  :                            271 .	which points TCP past consumed / duplicate
  :                            272 .	payload in incoming segments
  :                            273 
  :                            274 .	in high-volume space where certain TCP ports
  :                            275 .	have private buffer pools [HVBANK], the buffer
  :                            276 .	and its descriptor are in the same bank
  :                            277 
  :                            278 .	the descriptor is always located by a chain
  :                            279 .	head or previous descriptor pointing forward
  :                            280 .	to it
  :                            281 
  :                            282 .	in the private buffer bank arrangement TCP
  :                            283 .	inbound segment descriptors are located with
  :                            284 .	the pointer previous:sobdi.previous:next
  :                            285 
  :                            286 .	descriptor:sobdi is part of a next pointer
  :                            287 .	and does not concern the buffer of the descriptor
  :                            288 
  :                            289 .	sobdi is a reuse of apdata, which points to
  :                            290 .	the trailing edge of tcp segment header /
  :                            291 .	leading edge of tcp segment payload. apdata
  :                            292 .	is only needed during consumption and
  :                            293 .	construction of datagrams, and in high-volume
  :                            294 .	private buffer arrangements is replaced
  :                            295 .	during input and output by sobdi, which is
  :                            296 .	the bank base of the socket's private buffer 
  :                            297 .	pool
  :                            298 
  :                            299 .	during operations within TCP the bank containing
  :                            300 .	the segment is in address space whenever the socket
  :                            301 .	is in address space	
  :                            302 
  :                            303 .	high-volume private buffer pools are only used
  :                            304 .	in some TCP traffic which is expected to be of
  :                            305 .	extreme volume or confidential. The private buffer
  :                            306 .	arrangement enables millions of transmitted segments
  :                            307 .	awaiting remote acknowledgement to remain buffered
  :                            308 
  :                            309 .	interface device drivers drop transmitted datagrams
  :                            310 .	from protocols which do not resend
  :                            311 
  :                            312 .	TCP flags bit 23 of descriptor:bytes and interface
  :                            313 .	drivers do not drop those buffers after transmission
  :                            314 
  :                            315 
00:002D8E 70B000              +316 q2if	tz	queue:head
00:002D8F B60D94              +317 	j	q2ifz
00:002D90 20B000              +318 	sa	queue:head
00:002D91 20B001              +319 	sa	queue:tail
00:002D92 30C007              +320 	z	descriptor:ifql, a
00:002D93 350000              +321 	lret	0
  :                            322 
00:002D94 68B001              +323 q2ifz	lb	queue:tail
00:002D95 20D007              +324 	sa	descriptor:ifql, b
00:002D96 20B001              +325 	sa	queue:tail
00:002D97 30C007              +326 	z	descriptor:ifql, a
00:002D98 350000              +327 	lret	0
  :                            328 
00:002D99 60B000              +329 readqif	la	queue:head
00:002D9A C60D9D              +330 	jza	rqifz
00:002D9B 68C007              +331 	lb	descriptor:ifql, a
00:002D9C 28B000              +332 	sb	queue:head
00:002D9D 350000              +333 rqifz	lret	0
  :                            334 
  :                            335 
  :                            336 	$if	HVBANK
  :                            337 
  :                            338 .	these macros work in A$TXBUFFB which is TCP outgoing
  :                            339 .	segments private buffer window, and cannot be used
  :                            340 .	to manage any other memory window 
  :                            341 
  :                            342 .	the descriptor + buffer returned at hvreadifq are
  :                            343 .	placed in memory window A$TXBUFFB
  :                            344 
  :                            345 .	the descriptor + buffer queued by q2ifhv are not
  :                            346 .	afterwards guaranteed in the address space, but
  :                            347 .	their long address is still in a:b
  :                            348 
  :                            349 q2ifhv	tz	1, y
  :                            350 	j	q2ifhv_tail
  :                            351 	ds	hvqueue:head 
  :                            352 	ds	hvqueue:tail
  :                            353 	lret	0
  :                            354 q2ifhv_tail
  :                            355 	dpush	a
  :                            356 	dpush	a
  :                            357 	dl	hvqueue:tail		. get qtail word offset pointer
  :                            358 	sabr	A$TXBUFFB			. get *qtail in address space
  :                            359 	pop	descriptor:sobdi, b	. write new qtail bank pointer
  :                            360 	pop	descriptor:ifql,b	. write new qtail word offset pointer
  :                            361 	pop	a			. take 2nd copy descriptor pointer
  :                            362 	pop	b			. from the internal stack
  :                            363 	ds	hvqueue:tail
  :                            364 	lret	0
  :                            365 
  :                            366 hvreadqif
  :                            367 	dl	hvqueue:head
  :                            368 	jzb	hvreadqifz		. queue empty
  :                            369 .	sabr	A$TXBUFFB			. interface already based the descriptor at qhead
  :                            370 	push	descriptor:sobdi, b	. next pointer to queue head
  :                            371 	push	descriptor:ifql, b
  :                            372 	pop	1, y
  :                            373 	pop	0, y
  :                            374 hvreadqifz
  :                            375 	lret	0
  :                            376 
  :                            377 .	these macros use descriptor:next pointer like readq
  :                            378 .	and q2, and are used in upper protocols
  :                            379 
  :                            380 .	descriptor:sobdi is the containing bank pointer of
  :                            381 .	the descriptor + buffer at descriptor:next
  :                            382 
  :                            383 .	these macros work in A$BUFFA which is TCP incoming
  :                            384 .	segments private buffer window, and cannot be used
  :                            385 .	to manage any other memory window
  :                            386 
  :                            387 .	the descriptor + buffer returned from hvreadq are
  :                            388 .	based in window A$BUFFA
  :                            389 
  :                            390 .	the descriptor + buffer queued by q2hv are not
  :                            391 .	afterwards guaranteed in the address space
  :                            392 .	but their long address is still in a:b
  :                            393 
  :                            394 q2hv	tz	1, y
  :                            395 	j	q2hv_tail
  :                            396 	ds	hvqueue:head, y
  :                            397 	ds	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            398 	lret	0
  :                            399 q2hv_tail
  :                            400 	dpush	a
  :                            401 	dpush	a
  :                            402 	dl	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            403 	sabr	A$BUFFA
  :                            404 	pop	descriptor:sobdi, b
  :                            405 	pop	descriptor:next, b
  :                            406 	pop	a
  :                            407 	pop	b
  :                            408 	ds	hvqueue:tail, y
  :                            409 	lret	0
  :                            410 
  :                            411 hvreadq	dl	hvqueue:head, y
  :                            412 	jzb	hvreadqifz
  :                            413 	sabr	A$BUFFA
  :                            414 	push	descriptor:next_bdi, b
  :                            415 	push	descriptor:next, b
  :                            416 	pop	1, y
  :                            417 	pop	0, y
  :                            418 	lret	0
  :                            419 
  :                            420 
  :                            421 	$endif	HVBANK
  :                            422 	$endif	QSIMULTANEOUS

  :                            99 
  :                            100 ethernet_arp	$tree
  :                            101 
  :                            102 .	ARP for
  :                            103 .		48-bit physical addresses
  :                            104 .	and	32-bit protocol addresses
  :                            105 
  :                            106 .	this may be the only ARP needed
  :                            107 .	if another ARP is needed
  :                            108 .	for different size addresses
  :                            109 .	include arp.msm again
  :                            110 
  :                            111 .	netspace	$tree
  :                            112 .	SETTINGS	$set	values
  :                            113 .			$include arp
  :                            114 .			$root
  :                            115 .
  :                            116 .	whatever storage bank that is in
  :                            117 .	needs four vectors at the front
  :                            118 
  :                            119 .	netspa_arp_rx* $vector	netspace:arp_rx
  :                            120 .	netspa_arp_reso* $vector netspace:resolve
  :                            121 .	netspa_arp_xmit* $vector netspace:xmit
  :                            122 .	xarp_netspace*	$vector	netspace:xarp
  :                            123 
  :                            124 .	these vectors go in the interface descriptor
  :                            125 
  :                            126 .	this_if:arp_rx
  :                            127 .	this_if:arp_tx
  :                            128 .	this_if:arp_resolve
  :                            129 .	this_if:xarp
  :                            130 
  :                            131 .		ql	(netspa_arp_rx, netspa_arp_tx, netspa_arp_reso, xarp_netspace:q)
  :                            132 .		qs	this_if:arp_rx
  :                            133 
  :                            134 .	this would usually happen in ifconfigure.msm  where 
  :                            135 .	the vector pointer to the routine for constructing
  :                            136 .	link layer header is also written to this_if:write_llh
  :                            137 
  :                            138 
  :                            139 .	the settings and and generation of ethernet ARP follow here
  :                            140 
  :                            141 
  :                            142 ARP_STORE $set	50	. however many ARP cache rows are required
  :                            143 
  :                            144 HTYPE	$set	ETHERNET_SPACE
  :                            145 HLEN	$set	6
  :                            146 PLEN	$set	4
  :                            147 PROTOCOL_SUITE	$set	0800
  :                            148 LINK_PROTOCOL	$set	0806
  :                            149 
  :                            150 ARP_XMIT	$set	arp_ether_xmit
  :                            151 
  :                            152: 		$include	arp

  :                            173 
  :                            174 
  :                            175 .	the other 3 functions process two 192-bit inputs
  :                            176 
  :                            177 .	but 192-bit divide must test 
  :                            178 .	whether the divisor is represented in 1 or 2 floating numbers
  :                            179 
  :                            180 .	a 96-bit number sounds big
  :                            181 .	but if the divisor exponent is < 0 or > 31 decimal equivalent
  :                            182 .	then the 2nd divisor floating number is nonzero
  :                            183 .	for now deliver an indicative result in this case
  :                            184 .	by rounding both dividend and divisor to 96 bits
  :                            185 .	(adding their major and minor parts) and delivering a 96-bit quotient
  :                            186 
  :                            187 .	if the divisor is only one floating number (minor part is zero)
  :                            188 .	deliver a 192-bit quotient from a 192-bit dividend 
  :                            189 
  :                            190 fp_divide

  :                            194 
  :                            195 	$if	1
